Pull loop-through-loop often with the latch tool. Used for chain cast on (CH CO) and decorative techniques
Closed Cast On with the latch tool, pulling loop through loop over needles

Get your knitting started.

Software for pattern charting and stitch design
Machine Needlebed aligned so the needles are facing up (opposite the main bed)

Reduce the number of needles in work usually by doubling up stitches. example: Decrease at beg of row
Full Needle Rib (also refers to a weight of yarn)

example: Cast on over EVERY OTHER needle

ribber
Knitting multiple colors across a row. (Jacquard)
Using all needles on the ribber and main bed at the same time.

An accessory that allows quick removal of stitches from the needlebed. Commonly used to reverse the knitting.
Unit of measurement equal to .035 ounces
Often used to finish the edges of knitting
Pick up stitches to create double layer of knitting. See RHH | reverse hung a hem
Needle position E - D depending on brand (pulled all the way out)
A unit of measurement equal to 2.54 cm
Add to the number of stitches in work (multiple methods)
Knit 2 together is a hand knitting term referring to positioning stitches to lean to the right.  Used for decreases or creating eyelet lace.
Knitting carriage that forms stitches (can be 1 or 2 pieces) | Brother: K Carriage
Knitting sequence to prevent holes when working short rows

Cast on with M.Y. and knit 10 rows. Then change to Con Y. and continue knitting
back bed, knitter

Unit of measure

indicates carriage tension dial setting
Indicates carriage tension dial setting. If main tension is 5 - set the tension to 7
Indicates carriage tension dial setting. If main tension is 5 - set the tension to 4
indicates carriage tension dial setting
Indicates carriage tension dial setting. If main tension is 5 - set the tension to 4
Indicates carriage tension dial setting. If main tension is 5 - set the tension to 7
Indicates yarn colors see also CC (contrasting color) | Main Color MC
Knitting machine needles are specific to brand and model of machines. They consist of a hook, latch, stem, butt and shank.
example: Position every 3rd needle out of work
Needles that are in 'A' position and will not knit no matter what the carriage setting. See Out of Work Needles
Needles that are in "A" position and will not knit no matter what the carriage setting. See NWP Non-working position or NOW Needles out of Work
example: Transfer ribber stitches to the opposite needles on the  main bed
example: Opposite the carriage, put needles in hold
Needles that are in "A" position and will not knit no matter what the carriage setting. See NWP Non-working position or NOW Needles out of Work
Unit of weight measurement
Refers to the side of the knitting that faces a machine knitter (purl bumps show)
example: Work in pattern for 12 rows
A paper card with holes used with a punchcard patterning device to automatically select needles for stitch patterning.
Electronic patterning device for Silver Reed SK 830/840/860/890. Used in conjunction with the EC1 Pattern Controller
Hang a contrasting loop on the edge needle to easily locate a specific row or match 2 pieces for seaming
example: Place all needles in hp | hold position
Common abbreviation in hand knitting.
Often refers to re-hanging stitches from an open edge (live stitches)
Knit R (right)

main bed, knitter
Indication of the number of rows completed. Indicated by RC:100

example: Rep from *.* 3 times.
example: Return 3 needles to working position every other row 3 times.
example: Remove the work on a garter bar, turn to rev (reverse) and rehang the knitting
Pick up stitches to create double layer of knitting with the PURL side facing. see HH | hung hem
Refers to the side of the knitting that will be seen when the garment is worn. Opposed to the Wrong Side (private side) Public Side
A technique for reversing rows of knit and purl stitches using a garter bar, scrapping off or hand knitting needle
Japanese flat bed machines are commonly made up of 2 needlebeds facing each other (main bed and ribber). The single or main bed can be used independently.
Remove knitting on waste yarn, leaving live stitches.
Quickly "seam" knitted pieces as you knit.
often used to finish the edges of knitting
The device on the carriage that controls the flow of yarn and impacts the stitch size | Tension Dial
Knitting a only a few stitches in a row. Often used for intarsia and garment shaping | Partial Knitting
Can refer to the tension dial on the carriage
Crochet stitch often used to seam knitting pieces (slip is also a textured stitch technique)
Basic stitch formation on knitting machines. Equivalent to knit 1 row purl 1 row in flat hand knitting. (Stocking stitch, plain knitting)
example: Bind off 34 sts
Can refer to the tension dial on the carriage or the tension dial on the mast
The device on the carriage that controls the flow of yarn and impacts the stitch size. See Stitch Dial
example: Knit 3 together | Decrease
example: Work trs(transfers) 10 times.
Needle position C or D (depending on brand)
Also called scrap knitting
Needle position that allows stitches to be formed. Depends on the carriage setting. Opposite of out-of-work needle
method for measuring yarn thickness
Refers to e-wrapping a separate strand around the end needle as the piece is knit.  Apparently used to control the edge curl, or give the edge more stability.
Refers to the side of the knitting that will be against the body. Opposed to the Right Side (public side)
Used to temporarily hold stitches. Should be a similar weight to the garment yarn and a contrasting color if possible (scrap yarn)
Repeat this row 2X (2 times)
Controlled holes used to make lace, small buttonholes and specialized stitch techniques. [yo] is a hand knitting term
A method often used by machine knitters to identify and compare yarn weights
